worth_it_explanation: Amazon DSP presents four goal types at the campaign level, reach, consideration, conversions, and retail outcomes, and each one changes the default optimization signal the platform uses. A campaign built for reach that is also tracking purchase events does not automatically pivot to purchase optimization. The goal type has to match the business objective, and the key performance indicator (KPI) selected at the line item has to reinforce it. This check is a configuration review, not a deep analysis, and the misalignments it surfaces are the kind that drain budget for months without an obvious signal.
intro: Amazon DSP organizes campaign objectives into four categories: reach, consideration, conversions, and retail outcomes. The goal type selected at the campaign level shapes which optimization signals the platform favors and which key performance indicators (KPIs) the reporting surfaces. These checks confirm that the goal type matches what the advertiser actually needs to move, that the KPI follows from the goal rather than inheriting a default, and that awareness and performance objectives are not combined in one line item where they pull bidding in opposing directions.

audit_checks.title: Campaign goal type does not match the business objective
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the goal type selected in Amazon DSP does not match the outcome the advertiser needs, the platform optimizes toward the wrong signal. Reach campaigns prioritize unique users; conversion campaigns prioritize purchase events. Running a reach configuration against a direct-response objective means the optimization engine is pulling in a direction the business does not need, and the KPIs it surfaces will not reflect what actually matters.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP assigns a goal type at the campaign level. The four options are reach, consideration, conversions, and retail outcomes. The goal type is not cosmetic. It signals to the platform which optimization path to take at the line item and surfaces the corresponding KPIs in reporting.

A reach campaign optimizes for unique reach and frequency distribution. A consideration campaign optimizes for click-through rate (CTR) and detail page views (DPVs). A conversions campaign optimizes for the conversion event configured on the order. A retail outcomes campaign targets Amazon-specific signals such as purchase rate, new-to-brand (NTB) orders, or return on ad spend (ROAS) drawn from Amazon's purchase graph.

The distinction between conversions and retail outcomes matters for endemic advertisers. A conversions campaign uses the Amazon Ad Tag pixel and suits non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon destinations. A retail outcomes campaign uses Amazon's own purchase data and suits advertisers whose products live on Amazon. Choosing conversions for an endemic advertiser means the campaign cannot access purchase signals that are already available in Amazon's retail data.

A few signs point to a goal type mismatch:
  • A campaign intended to drive product purchases on Amazon is set to reach or consideration
  • An endemic advertiser's campaign is set to conversions rather than retail outcomes
  • The KPIs the account team is reporting to the client do not align with what the goal type surfaces in Amazon DSP reporting
You may want to compare the advertiser's stated business objective against the goal type on each campaign before reviewing anything else. A mismatch at the campaign level makes the rest of the configuration harder to evaluate.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic advertiser selling on Amazon, a retail outcomes goal type gives the campaign access to Amazon's purchase data, including NTB orders, purchase rate, and ROAS. You may want to confirm that endemic campaigns are using retail outcomes rather than conversions. The conversions goal type relies on the Amazon Ad Tag pixel and does not surface the same retail signals.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser driving to an off-Amazon destination such as a lead form or a product detail page on the brand's own site, a conversions goal type is appropriate since it relies on the Amazon Ad Tag pixel placed on the advertiser's site. You may want to confirm the pixel is active and the conversion event is configured before assuming the campaign is optimizing toward anything real.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform
Maximize campaign performance with the Amazon Owned and Operated Network

investigate.intro: Open each active campaign in the Amazon DSP console and confirm the goal type against the advertiser's stated business objective.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Navigate to the Campaigns view and open each campaign you are reviewing.
Locate the goal type field in the campaign settings. Note whether it is set to reach, consideration, conversions, or retail outcomes.

Compare the goal type to the advertiser's primary objective for that campaign. For an endemic advertiser, confirm that campaigns intended to drive Amazon purchases are set to retail outcomes, not conversions. For a non-endemic advertiser, confirm that campaigns driving to off-Amazon destinations are set to conversions.
Note any campaign where the goal type and the stated objective point in different directions. Record the campaign name and the goal type that was set.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active campaign, the goal type set in the Amazon DSP console and the advertiser's stated business objective for that campaign. A campaign whose goal type matches its objective passes. A campaign where goal type and objective diverge, for example a retail outcomes objective with a reach goal type, or an endemic campaign set to conversions, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the goal type in use.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the goal type and stated objective for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} are running under a goal type that does not match the business objective. Amazon DSP routes optimization signals based on the goal type set at the campaign level, so a campaign configured for reach will not automatically pivot toward purchase events even when those events are being tracked. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the goal type on each affected campaign and updating it to match the actual objective, retail outcomes for endemic advertisers driving Amazon purchases, conversions for non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon destinations, and reach or consideration only when upper-funnel exposure is the genuine goal.

audit_checks.title: Line item KPI does not follow from the campaign goal type
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Amazon DSP surfaces a set of available KPIs at the line item based on the campaign's goal type. When a practitioner overrides the default with a KPI that belongs to a different goal, the optimization signal the platform uses diverges from the one the campaign was built for. The account may appear to be optimizing when it is running against a metric that the goal type is not actually able to move.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP allows a KPI to be set at the line item level within a campaign. The available KPIs vary by goal type. A reach campaign surfaces cost per thousand impressions (CPM) and frequency-related metrics. A consideration campaign surfaces CTR and cost per click (CPC). A conversions campaign surfaces cost per action (CPA) and purchase rate. A retail outcomes campaign surfaces ROAS, NTB purchase rate, and detail page view rate (DPVR).

The KPI informs how the line item bids in the auction. When the KPI at the line item reinforces the goal type, the optimization runs in a consistent direction. When it does not, the line item may be optimizing toward a proxy metric that the campaign structure is not built to support.

A common example is a retail outcomes campaign where the line item KPI is left at the CPM default. The campaign is set up to drive purchases, but the line item is bidding to minimize cost per impression rather than to reach users likely to buy. The result is efficient reach, not efficient retail outcomes.

A few signs point to a KPI that does not follow from the campaign goal:
  • A retail outcomes campaign has line items still set to CPM or CTR
  • A conversions campaign has line items set to DPVR, which is a consideration metric
  • The KPI being reported to stakeholders is not the one configured on the line item
You may want to confirm the KPI on each line item matches the campaign's goal type before reviewing creative or targeting. An optimization signal that conflicts with the goal makes the rest of the setup harder to assess.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, retail outcomes campaigns should have line items set to a retail KPI such as ROAS or NTB purchase rate. A line item on a retail outcomes campaign that is set to CPM or CTR is not drawing on the purchase signals Amazon DSP has access to for that goal type. You may want to update the KPI before concluding that the campaign is optimizing for retail performance.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers using a conversions campaign, the line item KPI should be CPA or purchase rate tied to the configured Amazon Ad Tag conversion event. A line item set to CPM on a conversions campaign is not optimizing toward the off-Amazon action. You may want to confirm both the KPI and the conversion event before treating the line item as conversion-optimized.

learn_more.source_title: What is a demand-side platform? A complete guide
Amazon Marketing Cloud: Discover advertising insights

investigate.intro: Open each active line item and confirm the KPI is set to a metric that aligns with the campaign's goal type.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Navigate to the Line Items view within each active campaign you are reviewing.
Open each line item and locate the KPI field. Note the metric it is set to.

Compare the KPI to the campaign's goal type. For a retail outcomes campaign, the KPI should be a retail metric such as ROAS or NTB purchase rate, not CPM or CTR. For a conversions campaign, the KPI should be CPA or purchase rate, not DPVR. For a reach campaign, CPM is appropriate.
Record any line item where the KPI does not follow from the campaign goal type, noting the campaign name, line item name, campaign goal, and the KPI in use.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active line item, the campaign goal type and the KPI set on the line item. A line item whose KPI reinforces the campaign goal type passes. A line item whose KPI belongs to a different goal, for example a CPM KPI on a retail outcomes campaign, is a fail recorded with the line item name, the campaign goal, and the KPI in use.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign goal type and line item KPI for each line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more line items on {{site_name}} are set to a KPI that does not follow from the campaign goal type. Amazon DSP uses the line item KPI to guide bidding, so a retail outcomes campaign with a cost per thousand impressions (CPM) KPI is optimizing for efficient reach rather than for the purchase signals the goal type was built to target. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the KPI on each affected line item and updating it to a metric that reinforces the campaign goal, such as ROAS or new-to-brand purchase rate for retail outcomes campaigns, or cost per action (CPA) for conversions campaigns.

audit_checks.title: Awareness and performance objectives are mixed in one line item
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A line item that carries both reach-optimized placements and conversion-optimized placements receives conflicting bidding signals. The platform cannot bid aggressively for reach and aggressively for conversion at the same time in the same auction. One objective can crowd out the other, and neither gets the budget allocation or optimization focus it would receive in a dedicated line item.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP allows creative formats and placements with very different optimization profiles to coexist in a single line item. Streaming TV (STV) inventory, for example, is a non-clickable format suited to reach and brand awareness. Display and mobile inventory is clickable and suited to consideration, conversions, and retail outcomes. When both format types run under one line item with one KPI, the platform allocates budget across all of them using a single optimization signal.

The conflict this creates is practical. A line item set to ROAS will favor placements where purchase signals are available and conversions have historically occurred. STV inventory does not generate purchase events. The platform may underspend on STV because it cannot demonstrate ROAS there, or it may overspend on lower-funnel placements and neglect the awareness goal the STV inventory was included to serve.

The inverse is also true. A line item set to CPM for reach will allocate budget to STV placements efficiently, but any display inventory in the same line item will be bought on a reach basis even when those placements have a purchase signal available.

A few signs point to awareness and performance objectives being mixed in one line item:
  • A line item contains both STV supply and standard display or mobile supply
  • The line item KPI is a retail or conversion metric, but STV placements are present in the supply settings
  • The campaign reports low STV delivery alongside underperformance on conversion KPIs
You may want to separate awareness-oriented placements from performance-oriented placements into dedicated line items, each with a KPI matched to the supply type it targets.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, STV placements are part of a brand-building strategy aimed at driving awareness of products on Amazon. Running STV alongside display in one line item with a ROAS KPI can suppress STV delivery since that inventory does not produce purchase events. You may want to separate the STV placements into a reach-goal line item so the awareness budget runs without competing against the performance budget.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, STV inventory reaches audiences in a lean-back context that does not support clicks. A conversions line item that includes STV supply will likely underdeliver on STV because the format cannot generate the conversion events the KPI requires. You may want to place STV in a separate reach-goal line item and reserve the conversions line item for clickable display and mobile formats.

learn_more.source_title: Maximize campaign performance with the Amazon Owned and Operated Network
Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Review each active line item's supply settings and KPI to identify cases where formats with different optimization profiles are competing for the same budget under one objective.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Navigate to the Line Items view within each active campaign.
Open each line item and review the supply settings to identify which supply types are included: STV, display, mobile, or a combination.
Note the KPI set on the line item. Confirm whether it is a reach-oriented metric (CPM) or a performance metric (ROAS, CPA, NTB purchase rate).

Flag any line item that contains both STV supply and clickable display or mobile supply with a single performance KPI, or conversely any line item that contains clickable supply with a pure reach KPI where conversion data is available.
Record the line item name, the supply types present, and the KPI in use for each flagged item.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active line item, the supply types included and the KPI. A line item with a single, consistent supply type and a KPI that matches passes. A line item that mixes STV with clickable display or mobile supply under one performance KPI, or any similar combination where the supply types require different optimization signals, is a fail recorded with the line item name, supply types present, and the KPI in use.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the supply types and KPI for each line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more line items on {{site_name}} combine supply types that require different optimization signals, most often streaming TV (STV) inventory alongside clickable display or mobile placements, under a single key performance indicator (KPI). Amazon DSP optimizes each line item toward one KPI, so STV placements in a line item set to ROAS will compete for budget against display placements where purchase signals are available, and the STV delivery is likely to be suppressed because it cannot generate the conversion events the KPI expects. {{voice_recommend}} separating awareness-oriented supply, particularly STV, into dedicated reach-goal line items and reserving performance-goal line items for clickable formats where the chosen KPI can be measured.
